title: Run a family of TURF reach curves from multiple anchor sizes in parallel
summary: Generate a family of TURF reach curves by running an anchored ladder for each anchor size in parallel. Useful for assessing how 'best subset shape' depends on the anchor size. Writes per-anchor ladders, a combined value-points CSV, and the curve family details to out_dir.
description: Runs `search-ladder` once per anchor size in `anchor_ks` (defaulting to all feasible sizes), in parallel up to `parallel_jobs`, and combines the results into a single value-points CSV alongside the per-anchor ladders. Useful for sensitivity analysis: a single ladder anchored at one `k` can be misleading because the best subset at one size is not always a subset of the best at another. The family of ladders surfaces this disagreement.
usage:
[["maxdiff-turf::curve-family"]]
utilities = "<utilities.csv>"
out_dir = "<dir>"
# optional:
anchor_ks = [4, 6, 8, 10, 12]
subgroups = "<subgroups.csv>"
labels = "<labels.csv>"
include = "1,4"
exclude = "2,3"
utility_cols = "3-12"
non_utility_cols = "1,2"
forbidden_pairs = "1:2,3:4"
reach_method = "weighted-by-probability"
search_method = "auto"
anchor_top_n = 25
ladder_mode = "repair"
parallel_jobs = 4
seed = 42

arguments.name: utilities
arguments.description: Path to a per-respondent utilities CSV. Same format as every other maxdiff-turf command.

arguments.name: out_dir
arguments.description: Output directory for results. Created if it does not exist. Receives one ladder file per anchor, a combined value-points CSV (one row per (anchor, k, candidate)), and a top-level summary describing the family run.

arguments.name: anchor_ks
arguments.description: Anchor sizes to run ladders from. When omitted (or empty), defaults to all feasible sizes — i.e., every integer from 1 to the eligible item count. Pick a small list (e.g., `[4, 6, 8, 10, 12]`) for a coarse sensitivity sweep, or leave unset for a complete family.

arguments.name: subgroups
arguments.description: Path to an optional subgroup-membership CSV. When supplied, per-subgroup reach is reported alongside overall reach for every (anchor, k) pair.

arguments.name: labels
arguments.description: Path to an optional item-labels CSV used to render candidate subsets in human-readable form.

arguments.name: include
arguments.description: Comma-separated 1-based item indices to force-include at every step of every ladder.

arguments.name: exclude
arguments.description: Comma-separated 1-based item indices to exclude from the entire family run.

arguments.name: utility_cols
arguments.description: Explicit utility column spec, 1-based, supports ranges like `3-12`.

arguments.name: non_utility_cols
arguments.description: Comma-separated 1-based columns to ignore (id, weight, demographic columns).

arguments.name: forbidden_pairs
arguments.description: Forbidden-pair spec, e.g. `1:2,3:4`.

arguments.name: headers
arguments.description: Whether the input CSVs have header rows. Default `true`.

arguments.name: reach_method
arguments.description: Reach scoring rule used in every ladder.

arguments.name: number_of_alt
arguments.description: Number of alternatives in the implied choice task. Default `5`.

arguments.name: threshold
arguments.description: Threshold value for the threshold reach method. Ignored unless `reach_method = "threshold"`.

arguments.name: search_method
arguments.description: Anchor search strategy used at the top of each ladder. `auto` (default) picks per anchor based on `C(n, anchor_k)`.

arguments.name: exhaustive_limit
arguments.description: Anchor exhaustive enumeration limit. Used per ladder.

arguments.name: anchor_top_n
arguments.description: Number of anchor candidates kept per `anchor_k` before ladder construction. Each anchor's ladder seeds from this many candidates.

arguments.name: ladder_mode
arguments.description: Ladder construction mode. See `search-ladder` for details. Used for every ladder in the family.

arguments.name: beam_width
arguments.description: Beam width during ladder traversal when `ladder_mode = "beam"`.

arguments.name: step_top_n
arguments.description: Candidates kept per ladder step.

arguments.name: local_improve
arguments.description: Apply local improvement at every ladder step. Default `true`.

arguments.name: local_improve_max_iter
arguments.description: Max iterations for local improvement at each step.

arguments.name: sample_n
arguments.description: Sampled-search budget. Overrides `sample_factor` when set.

arguments.name: sample_factor
arguments.description: Multiplier on the auto-sampled budget. Default `20`.

arguments.name: sample_replicates
arguments.description: Sampled-search replicates per anchor.

arguments.name: drop_frac
arguments.description: Fraction of items dropped each screening pass.

arguments.name: min_survivors
arguments.description: Minimum survivors retained during screening rounds.

arguments.name: screen_metric
arguments.description: Screening metric used during sampled search.

arguments.name: top_alpha
arguments.description: Top-alpha tail probability for screening.

arguments.name: seed
arguments.description: Random seed for the family run. Threaded through every per-anchor ladder; with the same seed and the same input the entire family is reproducible.

arguments.name: parallel_jobs
arguments.description: Number of anchors evaluated in parallel. Each anchor runs its own `search-ladder` worker; they do not share state. Set this to roughly the number of cores you can spare. Memory scales linearly with `parallel_jobs` because each worker keeps its own respondent matrix in memory.

arguments.name: keep_diagnostics
arguments.description: Retain diagnostic columns at every ladder step.

arguments.name: target_subgroup
arguments.description: Name of a single subgroup column to optimise against, instead of overall reach.

details:
`curve-family` is the right tool for sensitivity analysis on the choice of anchor. A single ladder gives you a value curve, but the curve is biased toward the neighbourhood of its anchor — if the ladder were anchored elsewhere, both the curve shape *and* the specific items chosen at any given size could differ. The family pass reveals this by running ladders from several different anchors and surfacing where they agree and where they disagree.

Each anchor's ladder is independent. The family run distributes them across `parallel_jobs` workers, each with its own copy of the prepared utilities matrix in memory. There is no cross-anchor sharing during the run; aggregation happens at the end, when the per-anchor outputs are merged into a single value-points CSV.

The combined value-points CSV is the main thing you'll plot: one row per (anchor, k, candidate), so you can colour by anchor and see how reach-vs-k differs across the family. When all ladders agree on the best subset at every size, you can trust a single ladder. When they diverge, the divergence is itself the answer to a useful question: it means the local optimum landscape is multi-modal, and you should pick the anchor whose ladder corresponds to the size you actually care about.

If you only need a single value curve and aren't worried about anchor bias, use `search-ladder` directly — it's much cheaper.
value:
On success, `out_dir` contains:

- One ladder CSV per entry in `anchor_ks` (or per feasible anchor when `anchor_ks` is unset).
- A combined value-points CSV with one row per (anchor, k, candidate) — the file you plot to compare ladders.
- A top-level family summary describing the run (anchors used, parallelism, search settings).

In protocol mode the plugin emits `event.run.started`, one `event.artifact.created` per file written, and a final `event.run.finished` (or `event.run.failed`).
notes: When all ladders in the family agree on the per-size best subset, you can safely use a single `search-ladder` run from any anchor. The family pass is most informative when anchors disagree.
Memory scales linearly with `parallel_jobs` because each worker holds its own respondent matrix. For very large inputs, lower `parallel_jobs` rather than reducing search quality.
If you only care about specific sizes (e.g., for a stakeholder report), `search-ladder` with `refine_sizes` is usually a better fit than a full family run.

see_also.description: Single-anchor ladder — the per-anchor unit that this command parallelises.
Score hand-built shortlists against the same utilities, useful for benchmarking the family.

examples.title: Family across a hand-picked set of anchors
Family optimised for a specific subgroup with forbidden pairs
examples.body:
[["maxdiff-turf::curve-family"]]
utilities = "./utilities.csv"
subgroups = "./subgroups.csv"
labels = "./labels.csv"
out_dir = "./family-subgroup"
anchor_ks = [4, 6, 8, 10]
target_subgroup = "segment_a"
forbidden_pairs = "3:5"
parallel_jobs = 4
seed = 99

fields.name: utilities
fields.description: Path to per-respondent utilities CSV.

fields.name: out_dir
fields.description: Output directory for results. Created if it does not exist.

fields.name: anchor_ks
fields.description: Anchor sizes (array of integers). Empty = all feasible sizes.

fields.name: subgroups
fields.description: Optional subgroup membership CSV.

fields.name: labels
fields.description: Optional item labels CSV.

fields.name: include
fields.description: Comma-separated 1-based item indices to force-include.

fields.name: exclude
fields.description: Comma-separated 1-based item indices to exclude.

fields.name: utility_cols
fields.description: Explicit utility column spec (1-based, supports ranges).

fields.name: non_utility_cols
fields.description: Comma-separated 1-based columns to ignore.

fields.name: forbidden_pairs
fields.description: Forbidden pairs spec, e.g. '1:2,3:4'.

fields.name: headers
fields.description: Whether the CSV files have header rows. Default: true.

fields.name: reach_method
fields.description: Reach scoring rule.

fields.name: number_of_alt
fields.description: Implied alternatives per task. Default: 5.

fields.name: threshold
fields.description: Threshold value for the threshold reach method. Default: 0.8.

fields.name: search_method
fields.description: Anchor search strategy.

fields.name: exhaustive_limit
fields.description: Anchor exhaustive enumeration limit. Default: 100000.

fields.name: anchor_top_n
fields.description: Number of anchor candidates kept per anchor_k before ladder construction. Default: 25.

fields.name: ladder_mode
fields.description: Ladder construction mode.

fields.name: beam_width
fields.description: Beam width during ladder traversal. Default: 25.

fields.name: step_top_n
fields.description: Candidates kept per ladder step. Default: 10.

fields.name: local_improve
fields.description: Apply local improvement pass to candidates. Default: true.

fields.name: local_improve_max_iter
fields.description: Max iterations for local improvement. Default: 25.

fields.name: sample_n
fields.description: Sampled-search budget.

fields.name: sample_factor
fields.description: Multiplier on the auto-sampled budget. Default: 20.

fields.name: sample_replicates
fields.description: Sampled-search replicates. Default: 3.

fields.name: drop_frac
fields.description: Fraction of items dropped each screening pass. Default: 0.25.

fields.name: min_survivors
fields.description: Minimum survivors retained during screening rounds.

fields.name: screen_metric
fields.description: Screening metric used during sampled search.

fields.name: top_alpha
fields.description: Top-alpha tail probability for screening. Default: 0.10.

fields.name: seed
fields.description: Random seed (omit for non-deterministic).

fields.name: parallel_jobs
fields.description: Number of anchors evaluated in parallel. Default: 1.

fields.name: keep_diagnostics
fields.description: Retain diagnostic columns. Default: true.

fields.name: target_subgroup
fields.description: Optimize against a specific subgroup column.

artifacts.title: Per-anchor ladder CSV
artifacts.description: One ladder file per anchor size, showing the reach curve when the search is anchored at that subset size. Lets you see whether the recommended portfolio changes depending on the starting point.

fields.name: anchor_k
fields.description: The anchor size for this ladder.

fields.name: k
fields.description: Subset size at this step.

fields.name: reach
fields.description: TURF reach at this (anchor, k) combination.

artifacts.title: Combined value-points CSV
artifacts.description: All ladders stitched into one file — one row per (anchor, k, candidate item). This is the file to load into a chart to compare how the reach curve shape shifts across anchor sizes.

fields.name: anchor_k
fields.description: The anchor size for this ladder family member.

fields.name: k
fields.description: Subset size.

fields.name: reach
fields.description: TURF reach.

fields.name: item
fields.description: Item at this ladder step.

artifacts.title: Curve family summary
artifacts.description: Top-level metadata about the run: which anchor sizes were used, parallelism settings, and search parameters. Useful for documenting the analysis.
